critic_raw:
Issue 1: Merchant self-selection — pilot group not comparable to rest of network.
Issue 2: Concurrent authentication changes explain fraud reduction independently.
Issue 3: False decline comparison confounded by different approval rates.
defender_raw: Round 1: On Issue 1 — concede. The merchant size and sophistication difference is a real confound that invalidates the causal claim. On Issue 2 — concede. Authentication changes are an independent fraud reduction mechanism; the concurrent adoption by some pilot merchants confounds attribution. On Issue 3 — concede. If pilot merchants had higher approval rates, false decline reduction may reflect threshold relaxation rather than model precision.

debate_rounds.critic: Full concession. Critique wins. The network-wide expansion recommendation requires a properly controlled study.
debate_rounds.defender: Round 1: Full concession. The pilot result is promising but not causally clean. A randomized within-merchant test with stable authentication is needed.

points_resolved: Issue 3: false decline / approval rate confound — critique_wins
all_issues_raised: Issue 3: false decline / approval threshold confound

empirical_test.condition: Randomized within-merchant test with stable authentication settings; track approval rates and fraud outcomes consistently
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Fraud improvement disappears after matching for merchant characteristics and controlling for authentication changes
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Within stable-auth comparable merchants, significant fraud reduction and false decline improvement attributable to model
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Some model contribution but magnitude unclear due to residual confounding
